title: Reconfigurable MDI-QKD and BB84 over 20 km optical channels via EOM-tailored weak coherent states
authors: Jaesung Lim, Yonggi Jo, Nam Hun Park, Zaeill Kim, Yong Sup Ihn
abstract: Measurement-device-independent quantum key distribution (MDI-QKD) is designed to eliminate detector side-channel vulnerabilities. However, its practical deployment remains experimentally demanding because it requires two-photon interference (TPI) between mutually phase-randomized optical states. In this study, we demonstrate a reconfigurable platform that supports both polarization encoded MDI-QKD and BB84 measurements utilizing the same optical hardware over 20 km optical fiber channels. Two mutually phase-randomized weak coherent states (WCSs) are generated from a shared continuous-wave (CW) laser via electro-optic phase modulation and subsequent etalon-based first-order sideband filtering. Channel indistinguishability is verified through Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) interference, combining time-resolved coincidence measurements and polarization mismatch scans, confirming a high degree of indistinguishability that robustly approaches the classical upper limit of 0.5 for WCSs. The transmitted states go through partial Bell-state measurement (BSM) to implement MDI-QKD. Here, the sytem can be directly reconfigured for BB84 simply by rotatinga single half-wave plate (HWP) by 22.5 degree in one arm of the module. This seamless reconfiguration drastically reduces hardware redundancy and enhances operational flexibility in dynamic network environments. These results indicate that EOM-based frequency engineering using a shared CW laser offers a highly practical route toward scalable and reconfigurable quantum communication systems.
summary: The paper introduces a reconfigurable Quantum Key Distribution (QKD) platform that uses electro-optic modulation on a single laser source to operate over 20 km of fiber. It successfully demonstrates high photon indistinguishability necessary for quantum interference, while also proving the ability to switch between MDI-QKD and BB84 protocols by adjusting only one half-wave plate. This significantly enhances hardware efficiency for future quantum networks.
main_problem: The primary goal was developing a practical, reconfigurable quantum key distribution (QKD) platform capable of switching between MDI-QKD and BB84 protocols using shared hardware to minimize system redundancy.
main_result: The authors demonstrated high indistinguishability ($V \approx 0.5$) over 20 km channels and achieved seamless reconfiguration between MDI-QKD and BB84 modes by rotating a single half-wave plate, confirming robust operational flexibility.
method: The system utilized electro-optic modulation (EOM) on a shared CW laser to generate two mutually phase-randomized weak coherent states. Performance was characterized using time-resolved Hong–Ou–Mandel (HOM) interference and by measuring Quantum Bit Error Rates (QBERs) in both protocols.
model_or_system: The platform is an EOM-based QKD system operating over 20 km optical fiber, designed to support both MDI-QKD (via partial Bell-state measurement) and BB84. Reconfigurability is achieved by manipulating a single Half-Wave Plate (HWP) in the central module.
key_observables: HOM interference visibility ($V$), Mutual Coherence Time ($	ext{T}_c$), Quantum Bit Error Rates (QBERs) for both MDI-QKD and BB84.
important_parameters: 20 km optical channel length; Visibility approaching the classical limit of 0.5; HWP rotation angle of $22.5^\circ$ for mode switching.
main_assumptions: The security during protocol switching relies on using a physical beam block to obstruct unused channels in BB84 mode.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the optical architecture, spectral filtering performance (sideband isolation), time-resolved TPI histograms confirming indistinguishability across detunings, and comparisons of QKD count rates across different modes.
paper_structure: The paper details the hardware setup using EOMs for state generation, validates channel indistinguishability via HOM interference measurements, demonstrates MDI-QKD implementation through partial BSM, and concludes by showing the simple reconfiguration to BB84 mode.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic QI as it presents a practical engineering solution for building versatile quantum communication hardware using established components like EOMs and CW lasers.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Experimental setup and spectral characterization of the reconfigurable QKD platform. (a) Schematic of the optical architecture. Two mutually phase-randomized weak coherent states (WCSs) are generated from a single CW laser using EOMs driven by independent 9.5 GHz RF sources, followed by sideband filtering. After propagating through a 20 km optical fiber spool, the signals enter Charlie’s partial Bell-state measurement module. The system is reconfigured between MDI-QKD and BB84 modes simply by toggling a specific HWP in channel 1 to either 0◦or 22.5◦. (b) The spectra of the WCS preparation. The leftmost panel shows the raw optical spectrum after EOM phase modulation. The middle panel...

FIG. 2. Experimental verification of phase randomization and two-photon indistinguishability. (a,b) First-order interference traces recorded at the H-polarized outputs without the 20 km fiber spool, obtained with (a) synchronized and (b) independent RF source clocks. (c–e) Time-resolved Hong-Ou-MAndel (HOM) coincidence histograms as a function of the detection time difference ∆t for frequency detunings of (c) 0 kHz, (d) 1 kHz, and (e) 2 kHz in Alice’s arm. The experimental data (red points) are well described by the theoretical fits (solid gray curves) based on Eq. (1). (f) A polarization mismatch scan demonstrating a four-period coincidence oscillation. The upper and lower bounds (gray...

FIG. 3. Evaluation of QKD performance in the MDI-QKD and BB84 operation modes. (a,b) Normalized single-photon count rates maesured in the BB84 mode, achieved by setting the internal HWP to 22.5◦, for the (a) Alice–Charlie and (b) Bob–Charlie links. The bars indicate the detection probabilities across the four polarization states (H, V, D, A) for each encoded input. (c) Normalized coincidence count rates for the |ψ+⟩and |ψ−⟩Bell-state projections in the MDI-QKD mode (HWP at 0◦). The data represent all 16 polarization combinations encoded between Alice and Bob, demonstrating distinct projection characteristics in the Z and X bases. All data points in both operational modes were accumulated...

Figure 3(c) shows the resulting normalized coincidence count rates evaluated via these metrics. We obtained ex- perimental MDI-QKD QBERs of EZ = (2.1 ± 0.7)% and EX = (27.6±0.5)% between Alice and Bob. These results demonstrate reasonable agreement with the correspond- ing theoretical expectations of 0% and 25%, respectively, confirming the robust cryptographic stability of the plat- form.
